title: Comprehensions

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Comprehensions; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial §§4.7 and 5.1-5.6: iteration, range, comprehensions, and sequence techniques; atomic topic: Comprehensions
sources.claim: Python comprehensions build list, set, or dict results from an iterable with expression and filter clauses whose nesting order follows the equivalent explicit loops.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python comprehensions build list, set, or dict results from an iterable with expression and filter clauses whose nesting order follows the equivalent explicit loops.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A dict comprehension maps valid names to normalized lengths with one readable filter, replacing a three-line append/update loop. Your goal is to choose between a comprehension and explicit loop and justify readability and memory implications. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Core collections and text; Functions, scope and recursion; Dictionaries and sets; for, while and loop control. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python comprehensions build list, set, or dict results from an iterable with expression and filter clauses whose nesting order follows the equivalent explicit loops. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Comprehensions allocate their result, nested clauses can become unreadable, side effects obscure intent, assignment-expression scope needs care, and generator expressions are the lazy alternative. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A comprehension is always faster and more memory-efficient than a loop, regardless of result size or complexity.

includes: Use list, set and dictionary comprehensions for bounded, readable transformations.
Choose between a comprehension and explicit loop and justify readability and memory implications.
excludes: Comprehensions allocate their result, nested clauses can become unreadable, side effects obscure intent, assignment-expression scope needs care, and generator expressions are the lazy alternative.

outcomes: Choose between a comprehension and explicit loop and justify readability and memory implications.

examples.title: Comprehensions: direct language case
examples.situation: A dict comprehension maps valid names to normalized lengths with one readable filter, replacing a three-line append/update loop.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A dict comprehension maps valid names to normalized lengths with one readable filter, replacing a three-line append/update loop.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use a comprehension for a bounded readable transformation; choose an explicit loop for complex effects and a generator expression when lazy consumption is required.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use a comprehension for a bounded readable transformation; choose an explicit loop for complex effects and a generator expression when lazy consumption is required.

examples.title: Comprehensions: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A complex transformation with error handling remains an explicit loop because compressing it would hide branches and diagnostics.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A complex transformation with error handling remains an explicit loop because compressing it would hide branches and diagnostics.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Comprehensions allocate their result, nested clauses can become unreadable, side effects obscure intent, assignment-expression scope needs care, and generator expressions are the lazy alternative.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A comprehension is always faster and more memory-efficient than a loop, regardless of result size or complexity.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose between a comprehension and explicit loop and justify readability and memory implications.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A comprehension is always faster and more memory-efficient than a loop, regardless of result size or complexity.
